questions.question: In a formal report, “The results {a1} carefully” sounds objective.
Academic methods sections often use passive forms such as “The samples {a1}.”
Use passive when the focus is on the result: “The policy {a1} yesterday.”
“The data were analyzed” creates a more {a1} tone than “We analyzed the data.”
If the actor is important, active is clearer: “Dr Lee {a1} the report.”
Too many passive clauses can make a text {a1}.
In casual speech, “John kicked the ball” is better than “The ball {a1} by John.”
In formal style, omit the agent when it is not important: “The form {a1}.”
“The experiment was conducted” is more formal than “The experiment {a1} conducted.”
Avoid get-passive in formal scientific writing: “The test {a1}.”
Official notices often say “Applications must {a1} by Friday.”
When responsibility matters, write “We made mistakes,” not “Mistakes {a1}.”

saved_test.name: Passive Voice Formality Style (All levels V3)
saved_test.description: Standard V3 package for Passive Voice Formality Style across A1-C2.
